soap_note.raw:
Here is a clinical note summarizing the conversation:  

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Andrew, a 59-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension, presented to the office today with an upper respiratory infection. He reported feeling tired and short of breath after working in the yard, which he attributed to catching a cold. He denied any history of seasonal allergies and reported that his symptoms worsened with exertion, particularly when lifting heavy objects. He also reported joint pain and fatigue, but denied any other symptoms such as fever, nausea, or vomiting.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's vital signs were within normal limits, with no fever. The physical exam revealed scattered ronchi bilaterally on lung exam, which cleared with cough. The patient had some edema of the lower extremities and pain to palpation of the elbows bilaterally.  

RESULTS

  The patient's chest x-ray was normal, with no evidence of pneumonia or airspace disease. His diabetic labs showed an elevated hemoglobin A1c of 8, which the doctor would like to see improved to 6 or 7.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient was diagnosed with a viral syndrome, likely a cold, and a COVID-19 test was ordered to rule out COVID-19. The doctor prescribed Robitussin for cough and encouraged the patient to take ibuprofen or Tylenol for fever. The patient's depression was assessed as stable, but the doctor offered to start a medication regimen or refer him to psychotherapy in the future. The patient's type 2 diabetes was managed with an increase in metformin to 1000mg twice daily, and a follow-up hemoglobin A1c was ordered in 4 months. The patient's high blood pressure was well-managed, and the doctor continued him on lisinopril 20mg daily. A lipid panel was ordered to monitor his cholesterol levels. The patient was instructed to follow up in 4 months and to contact the office if his symptoms worsened.

reference_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Upper respiratory infection.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Andrew Campbell is a 59-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension. He presents today with an upper respiratory infection.

The patient reports that he has been doing a bit of work out in the yard in the last week or so. He started to feel really tired and short of breath. The patient denies coughing up anything, but he feels like he will soon be coughing up phlegm. He denies having a fever, but he felt a little warm. He attributed this to exerting himself. He reports that his elbows hurt quite a bit. He notes his knees were pretty tired and he felt some tension around his knees. The patient attributes these symptoms to lifting heavy bags. He has not been wearing a mask as much recently. He believes that he caught his first cold and his symptoms have worsened. He has had both of his COVID vaccinations.

He denies any history of seasonal allergies.

Regarding his depression, he states it has been a crazy year and a half. He was a little concerned about that, but for the most part, he has been doing well with it. His wife got him into barre classes and this has been relaxing.

Regarding his diabetes, he has been monitoring his sugar levels while he is at work, but is not as consistent on Saturdays and Sundays. His diet has been pretty good for the most part, except for some house parties. They have not been elevated since his respiratory symptoms began.

The patient has been monitoring his blood pressure at home. He reports that he is very regular with monitoring his blood pressure during the week, though less consistently on weekends. He reports his blood pressure has been under control. He has continued to utilize lisinopril 20 mg, daily.

The patient denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Constitutional: Denies fever.
• Cardiovascular: Endorses dyspnea on exertion.
• Respiratory: Endorses shortness of breath and cough.
• Gastrointestinal: Denies nausea or diarrhea.
• Musculoskeletal: Endorses bilateral elbow and knee pain.
• Psychiatric: Endorses depression.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Respiratory: Scattered rhonchi bilaterally, clears with cough.
• Cardiovascular: No murmurs, gallops, or rubs. No extra heart sounds.
• Musculoskeletal: Edema in the bilateral lower extremities. Pain to palpation of the bilateral elbows.

RESULTS

X-ray of the chest is unremarkable. No airspace disease. No signs of pneumonia.

Hemoglobin A1c is elevated at 8.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Andrew Campbell is a 59-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension. He presents today with an upper respiratory infection.

Upper respiratory infection.
• Medical Reasoning: I believe he has contracted a viral syndrome. His chest x-ray was unremarkable and he has received both doses of the COVID-19 vaccination.
• Additional Testing: We will obtain a COVID-19 test to rule this out.
• Medical Treatment: I recommend he use Robitussin for cough, as well as ibuprofen or Tylenol if he develops a fever.

Depression.
• Medical Reasoning: He has been practicing barre classes and is doing well overall.
• Medical Treatment: I offered medication or psychotherapy, but the patient opted to defer at this time.

Diabetes type 2.
• Medical Reasoning: His blood glucose levels have been well controlled based on home monitoring, but his recent hemoglobin A1c was elevated.
• Additional Testing: We will repeat a hemoglobin A1c in 4 months.
• Medical Treatment: We will increase his metformin to 1000 mg twice daily.

Hypertension.
• Medical Reasoning: He has been compliant with lisinopril and his blood pressures have been well controlled based on home monitoring.
• Additional Testing: We will order a lipid panel.
• Medical Treatment: He will continue on lisinopril 20 mg once daily. This was refilled today.

Follow up: I would like to see him back in approximately 4 months.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

Here is a clinical note based on the conversation:  

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  The patient, a 52-year-old female with a past medical history significant for rheumatoid arthritis, atrial fibrillation, and reflux, presents for her annual exam. She reports doing well and has been adhering to her treatment plan, including walking, exercising, and doing yoga to manage her arthritis. She has not experienced any joint pain or stiffness over the past year. She is still taking methotrexate 2.5 mg once weekly without any issues. She reports palpitations associated with stress, which have been ongoing for the past year. She has been trying meditation and running with her dog to manage stress, but has not seen significant improvement. She reports no issues with her reflux, stating that cutting out soda and taking protonix 40 mg daily has helped to manage her symptoms.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's vital signs are within normal limits. The physical exam reveals a slight 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, which is not concerning. The lungs are clear, and the patient has no lower extremity edema. However, she has pain to palpation of the right elbow, with edema and erythema present.  

RESULTS

  The event monitor results show that the patient is in and out of atrial fibrillation, with a conversion pause. The autoimmune panel shows that the patient's rheumatoid arthritis is well-controlled on methotrexate.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient's rheumatoid arthritis is well-controlled on methotrexate 2.5 mg once weekly. She will continue on this medication and will need a refill. The patient's atrial fibrillation is a concern, and she will be referred to cardiology for a cardiac ablation to map out and burn the abnormal rhythm. The patient's reflux is well-managed on protonix 40 mg daily, and she will continue with dietary modifications to avoid coffee and spicy foods. The patient will follow up with cardiology for the cardiac ablation and will be seen in the office in 6 months for a follow-up exam.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Annual exam.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Andrea Roberts is a 52-year-old female with a past medical history significant for rheumatoid arthritis, atrial fibrillation, and reflux, who presents today for her annual exam. It has been a year since she was last seen.

The patient states she is doing well. She has been walking, exercising, and doing yoga, which has helped with her arthritis. She denies issues with stiffness, pain, or flare ups over the last year. The patient has continued utilizing methotrexate 2.5 mg once a week and denies issues with that.

In terms of her atrial fibrillation, she endorses continued palpitations. The last episode she had was about 1 week ago. She has noticed that when she starts to get stressed they start to flare up again. She has tried meditation and running with her dog for stress relief, however these have not been helpful. The patient had called last month and had an event monitor done.

Regarding her GERD, she has continued to utilize Protonix 40 mg a day. The patient has cut out soda, which seemed to help. She has not had a flare up in over 5 months.

The patient endorses nasal congestion because of her allergies. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, and vomitting.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Ears, Nose, Mouth and Throat: Endorses nasal congestion from allergies.
• Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain or dyspnea. Endorses palpitations.
• Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath.
• Musculoskeletal: Denies joint pain.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Slight 2/6 systolic ejection murmur.
• Musculoskeletal: No lower extremity edema. Edema and erythema of the right elbow. Pain to palpation of the right elbow.

RESULTS

Event monitor: Positive for conversion pause.

Autoimmune panel: Normal. Well controlled rheumatoid arthritis.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Andrea Roberts is a 52-year-old female with a past medical history significant for rheumatoid arthritis, atrial fibrillation, and reflux, who presents today for her annual exam. It has been a year since she was last seen.

Rheumatoid arthritis.
• Medical Reasoning: The patient has remained active and has been compliant with methotrexate once weekly. Her recent autoimmune panel was normal.
• Medical Treatment: She will continue on methotrexate 2.5 mg once weekly. This was refilled today.
• Specialist Referrals: If she needs a referral back to the rheumatologist, she will let me know.

Atrial fibrillation.
• Medical Reasoning: She continues to experience episodes of palpitations. Her most recent episode was about 1 week ago. She is in normal sinus rhythm today, but her recent cardiac event monitor demonstrated a conversion pause.
• Specialist Referrals: We will refer her to cardiology for a cardiac ablation.

GERD.
• Medical Reasoning: She has been compliant with dietary modifications and denies any episodes in approximately 5 months.
• Medical Treatment: Continue on Protonix 40 mg daily.
• Patient Education and Counseling: She could continue with dietary modifications and avoid known dietary triggers.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  The patient, a 62-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, type 2 diabetes, and kidney transplant, presented to the emergency room for follow-up. He reported that he had not been monitoring his blood sugar readings closely and felt woozy over the weekend, which prompted his wife to take him to the emergency room. He was found to have high blood sugar, but was not admitted to the hospital. He attributed the high blood sugar to not monitoring his diet closely due to recent family obligations, including his mother's hospitalization for a hip replacement. He reported feeling better after returning home and has been following his diet closely since then.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's physical exam was notable for a slight 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, which has been present in the past. He also had 1+ edema in his lower extremities. His vital signs were within normal limits, and his pulse ox, blood pressure, and heart rate were all normal.  

RESULTS

  The patient's blood sugar was 162, and his hemoglobin A1c was 8, which is slightly high. His kidney function was stable, and his immunosuppression medications were being managed by Dr. Reyes.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient was diagnosed with hyperglycemia, which was managed by increasing his Lantus dose to 20 units at night and ordering another hemoglobin A1c in a couple of months. His depression was assessed as stable, and he was encouraged to continue with his current strategies, including meditation. His kidney transplant was found to be stable, and he was referred back to Dr. Reyes for management of his immunosuppression medications. The patient was instructed to follow up with Dr. Reyes and to call the doctor if he needed anything.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

ER follow-up.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Albert Powell is a 62-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, type 2 diabetes, and kidney transplant. He is here today for an emergency room follow-up.

The patient states he was not staying on top of his blood sugar readings and felt " woozy " over the weekend. His wife was concerned that his levels were elevated and wanted to take him to the emergency room. He was not admitted to the hospital. He reports that his mother is in the hospital and he has been eating poorly. He was not monitoring his blood sugars and what he was eating. When they got home from the visit he felt a lot better. Since then, he has been following his diet pretty closely. He continues Lantus insulin at bedtime.

Regarding his kidney transplant and has been doing pretty well. The patient has been pretty diligent about taking immunotherapy medication and following orders. The last time he saw Dr. Reyes was about 3 weeks ago and everything seemed to be fine.

Regarding his depression, the patient states it has been about a year and a half, but he has been pretty good with that. He has his moments, but as long as he finds some time to relax in the afternoon and then it seems to work out. He thinks he would probably want to continue that at least for a couple more months and see how it goes. His wife got him into meditation recently and he finds that relaxing so he feels he would like to continue that for a couple more months and see how it goes.

The patient denies any chest pain, shortness of breath, lightheadedness or dizziness.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain or dyspnea.
• Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath.
• Neurological: Denies lightheadedness or dizziness.
• Psychiatric: Endorses depression.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Constitutional: in no apparent distress.
• Neck: No carotid bruits appreciable.
• Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: Grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur.
• Musculoskeletal: 1+ edema in the bilateral lower extremities.

RESULTS

Fasting glucose is elevated at 162.
Hemoglobin A1c is elevated at 8.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Albert Powell is a 62-year-old male with a past medical history significant for depression, type 2 diabetes, and kidney transplant. He is here today for an emergency room follow-up.

Hyperglycemia.
• Medical Reasoning: He was recently seen in the emergency department with elevated blood glucose levels. He admits to dietary indiscretion prior to this, but has since improved his diet. His recent blood glucose level was 162, and his recent hemoglobin A1c was 8.
• Additional Testing: Repeat hemoglobin A1c in a few months.
• Medical Treatment: Increase Lantus to 20 units at night.
• Patient Education and Counseling: I advised him to continue monitoring his blood glucose levels at home and report those to me, as we may need to make further adjustments to his medication.

Depression.
• Medical Reasoning: He reports personal stressors over the past year and a half but is doing well overall. He recently started meditating.
• Medical Treatment: He should continue his current management strategies. We will hold off on medication and therapy at this time.
• Patient Education and Counseling: The patient was encouraged to contact me if he needs anything.

Status post renal transplant.
• Medical Reasoning: His kidney function appears stable and he has been compliant with his immunotherapy medications.
• Medical Treatment: Continue seeing Dr. Reyes for management of his immunosuppression medication.
• Patient Education and Counseling: He knows to reach out to me if he needs anything.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Jerry, a 54-year-old male with a past medical history significant for osteoporosis and multiple sclerosis, presented for an annual exam. He reported a stressful summer with frequent traveling, which has led to insomnia. Despite trying various strategies, including melatonin, meditation, and stretching, he has not found relief from his sleep issues. He has been taking fosamax for osteoporosis without any issues, and his multiple sclerosis has been stable with no new symptoms or weakness. He has been taking his medication as prescribed and has not experienced any side effects.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination revealed weakness in the lower extremities, with the right leg being 4/5 and the left leg being 3/5. Reflexes were normal, and lung and heart sounds were clear. Arthritic changes were noted in the right knee.  

RESULTS

  The patient's right knee x-ray showed changes consistent with arthritis, but otherwise appeared normal.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient's osteoporosis is stable, and he will continue on fosamax 1 tablet per week. He will also continue to see his neurologist for his multiple sclerosis and take his prescribed medication as directed. A refill of fosamax was ordered for 11 tablets. The patient was advised to follow up with his neurologist as scheduled and to report any new symptoms or concerns to his primary care physician.

Here is a clinical note summarizing the conversation:  

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Mrs. Martinez, a 43-year-old female, presented to the office today with a complaint of pain in her distal radius, specifically towards her wrist. She reported that the pain began after playing tennis and hitting the top of the net with her arm, which caused a jarring sensation. She also mentioned that her arm swelled slightly and she experienced some pain when moving her wrist. She denied numbness in her hand.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  Physical examination revealed tenderness over the distal radius, with pain on palpation and movement of the wrist, particularly with flexion and extension of the thumb. The patient also reported pain with stress on the thumb.  

RESULTS

  X-ray of the arm was performed, which showed no fractures or abnormalities.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  Assessment: The patient has a strain and possible contusion to the muscle in the distal radius, likely caused by the traumatic event during tennis. There is no evidence of a fracture.  Plan: Conservative treatment will be initiated, including:  * Ice application for pain and swelling * Anti-inflammatory medication (Motrin 800mg three times a day with food) * Follow-up appointment in one week to reassess symptoms and adjust treatment as needed.  Note: The patient will be ordered to follow up in one week if symptoms do not improve.

Here is a clinical note based on the conversation:  

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Lawrence, a 62-year-old male with a past medical history significant for type I diabetes, congestive heart failure, depression, and reflux, presents with complaints of shortness of breath. He reports swelling in his legs, lethargy, and difficulty walking up stairs. He has been experiencing these symptoms for approximately 10 days. He also reports difficulty lying flat in bed and waking up with stiffness. He denies acute shortness of breath at night, but reports one episode about a week ago. He is on an insulin pump and reports good blood sugar control, but admits to occasional forgetfulness about monitoring his blood sugar on weekends. He has a history of depression and has been trying natural solutions, but is open to medication. He also reports a history of reflux and is currently on omeprazole.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's vital signs are within normal limits. The physical exam reveals a slight 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, crackles bilaterally at the bases, and 1+ pitting edema in the lower extremities. The jugular venous distension and carotid bruits are absent.  

RESULTS

  The chest x-ray shows no evidence of airspace disease, but the patient is retaining some fluid. The labs show no elevated white blood cell count, indicating no infection. The patient's oxygenation level is within normal limits.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient is diagnosed with an acute heart failure exacerbation. The plan is to start the patient on lasix 40mg/day and have him weigh himself daily. If his weight does not decrease or increases, he is to call the doctor. If he experiences more shortness of breath at rest, he is to call the doctor or 911 and go to the emergency room. The patient is also to follow up with his endocrinologist for his type I diabetes and have a recent eye exam. He is referred to psychiatry for talk therapy for his depression. The omeprazole is continued for his reflux. The patient is to call the doctor if he has any problems with his breathing.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Shortness of breath.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Lawrence Walker is a 62-year-old male with a past medical history significant for type 1 diabetes, congestive heart failure, depression, and reflux, who presents with complaints of shortness of breath.

The patient states he has had some shortness of breath and mild leg swelling which he thinks has to do with eating some salty foods at some house parties recently. His shortness of breath is primarily with exertion such as walking up the stairs. This has been going on for about 10 days now. He woke from sleep once with shortness of breath about a week ago. He also notes discomfort lying flat in bed and musculoskeletal stiffness in the morning. He states that it takes him a little while to get up.

Regarding his type 1 diabetes, he is using his insulin pump. He has been consistently monitoring his blood sugars during the working week, but notes that he checks less on the weekends, due to being busy. He is trying to avoid sugar but admits to an affinity for chocolate bars.

Regarding his depression, he states that it has been a crazy year and a half. He has tried some natural solutions. In the summer, he did daily outdoor stretching, which was great; but now that the weather has changed, he needs to find some alternatives. He is not ready to try any medications. He denies suicidal and homicidal ideation.

Regarding his GERD, he is taking his omeprazole. He has made some lifestyle modifications like cutting back on his red bull intake and other caffeine. Reducing his alcohol intake has been tough during the holiday season, but he is getting better about this.

The patient denies chest pain, fever, chills, cough, and abdominal pain.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Constitutional: Denies fevers, chills.  Endorses fatigue.
• Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain. Endorses dyspnea on exertion.
• Respiratory: Denies coughor wheezing. Endorses shortness of breath..
• Musculoskeletal: Endorses lower extremity edema.
• Psychiatric: Endorses depression.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Neck: No carotid bruits appreciable. No jugular venous distention.
• Respiratory: Rales are heard bilaterally at lung bases.
• Cardiovascular: 3/6 systolic ejection murmur.
• Musculoskeletal: 1+ pitting bilateral lower extremity edema.

RESULTS

Chest x-ray did not show any evidence of airspace disease.

Labs were all within normal limits, including the WBC.

ASSESSMENT

Lawrence Walker is a 62-year-old male with a past medical history significant for type 1 diabetes, congestive heart failure, depression, and reflux, who presents with complaints of shortness of breath.

PLAN

Acute heart failure exacerbation.
• Medical Reasoning: The patient is retaining fluid. He has noticed increased fluid retention following dietary indiscretion. He has experienced dyspnea on exertion for the past 10 days.
• Medical Treatment: Initiate Lasix 40 mg a day.
• Patient Education and Counseling: I advised the patient to monitor and log his daily weights. He will contact me if these continue to increase. He was instructed to call me or 911 if he experiences dyspnea at rest.

Type 1 diabetes.
• Medical Treatment: This is currently stable with use of an insulin pump.
• Additional Testing: I will order a hemoglobin A1c in 1 month.
• Medical Treatment: He will continue the insulin pump. The patient will follow up with his endocrinologist.
• Patient Education and Counseling: I encouraged the patient to have an eye exam.

Depression.
• Medical Reasoning: The patient has been doing well with outdoor stretching. He continues to decline the use of medications for this issue.
• Medical Treatment: I will refer him to psychiatry as his current management strategy is unsustainable due to the change in season.
• Patient Education and Counseling: We will defer initiating medication at this time.

Acid reflux.
• Medical Reasoning: His symptoms are stable with medication.
• Medical Treatment: Continue omeprazole 20 mg a day.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan. his endo

Here is a clinical note summarizing the conversation in four sections:  

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Ms. James, a 42-year-old female, presented to the office on [current date] with a 5-day history of right index finger pain. She reported that the pain began after a skiing accident on Saturday, where she lost control and jammed her index finger on an unknown object. She has taken ibuprofen for the pain, but it has not provided significant relief. She rates her pain as a 7 out of 10.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  Physical examination revealed tenderness to palpation over the right index finger, particularly over the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint. The patient reported pain with finger flexion, extension, and compression. X-rays of the right hand were obtained, which showed no fractures or dislocations. The DIP joint of the right index finger was found to be swollen and tender to palpation.  

RESULTS

  X-ray of the right hand showed no fractures or dislocations. The DIP joint of the right index finger was found to be swollen and tender to palpation.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  Assessment: The patient has a sprain of the DIP joint of the right index finger. Plan: The patient will be placed in a splint to immobilize the finger and reduce pain. She will be prescribed 15mg of meloxicam (Mobic) once daily for 14 days to reduce inflammation. She will be seen in two weeks to follow up on the progress of her injury and to determine if further treatment, including hand therapy, is necessary.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  The patient, a 34-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension, presents with a 6-day history of back pain. She reports that the pain started after walking for 30 minutes to an hour a day, and describes it as a sudden onset of back stiffness and pain. She has been taking ibuprofen and applying heat to the area, but the pain persists. She also reports lifting weights 3 times a week, including deadlifts, which may have contributed to the injury. She denies any trauma, numbness, tingling, or weakness in her lower extremities. Her blood pressure has been well-controlled on lisinopril, and she has been monitoring it at home.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's vital signs are within normal limits. The physical exam reveals pain to palpation on the right lateral aspect of the lumbar spine, with pain on flexion and extension. The straight leg raise is negative. The heart exam reveals a stable 2/6 systolic ejection murmur.  

RESULTS

  The patient's x-ray of the lumbar spine shows good boney alignment with no obvious fracture. Her labs, including white blood cell count, are within normal limits with no signs of infection.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient is diagnosed with a lumbar strain, likely caused by lifting weights or exercising. The plan is to prescribe meloxicam 15mg once a day, with instructions to ice and apply heat to the area. The patient is also referred to physical therapy for strengthening exercises of the back. The patient's hypertension is well-controlled on lisinopril 10mg daily, and she is instructed to continue taking it and monitor her blood pressure readings. A refill of lisinopril is ordered.

reference_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Back pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Ramirez is a 34-year-old female with a past medical history significant for hypertension, who presents today with back pain.

The patient reports she has been walking a lot lately, 30 minutes to an hour or so a day. While walking 6 days ago, she felt her back seize up on her. She is not sure what caused it but thinks she was walking a little faster than usual. She describes the sensation as a spasm. She has been taking ibuprofen and using heat on it but it is still pretty stiff. She denies any known trauma or injury to her back. She notes she has been weightlifting around 3 times a week. She did do deadlifts that day before her walk and thinks she may have been using her back more than her legs to lift. She locates the pain in her mid lower back. She denies any lower extremity numbness, tingling, and weakness.

Regarding her hypertension, she states she has been doing readings at home and that has been looking great. She has been watching her diet which has helped. She is taking lisinopril.

The patient endorses nasal congestion, which she attributes to her seasonal allergies.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Ears, Nose, Mouth and Throat: Endorses nasal congestion from seasonal allergies.
• Musculoskeletal: Endorses bilateral low back pain and stiffness.
• Neurological: Denies numbness.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Cardiovascular: Slight 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, stable.
• Musculoskeletal: Pain to palpation along the right lateral aspect of her lumbar spine. Pain with flexion and extension. Negative straight leg raise.

RESULTS

Back x-ray reveals good bony alignment with no obvious fracture.

Labs: All within normal limits.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Ramirez is a 34-year-old female with a past medical history significant for hypertension, who presents today with back pain.

Back pain.
• Medical Reasoning: She experienced a spasm-like pain in her back while walking approximately 6 days ago. She has also been lifting weights recently. Her lumbar spine x-ray was unremarkable and her recent labs were normal. I believe she has a lumbar strain.
• Medical Treatment: We will initiate meloxicam 15 mg once daily.
• Specialist Referrals: We will refer her to physical therapy to work on strengthening exercises.
• Patient Education and Counseling: She may apply ice and heat to the area.

Hypertension.
• Medical Reasoning: This is well controlled with lisinopril and dietary modifications based on home monitoring.
• Medical Treatment: She will continue lisinopril 10 mg daily. This was refilled today.
• Patient Education and Counseling: I encouraged her to continue with home monitoring and report any elevated blood pressures to me.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

  Mrs. Anderson, a 44-year-old female, presents with a 1-week history of left shoulder pain. The pain began after a gymnastics class, where she thinks she may have fallen into an awkward position. She has tried taking Tylenol for 2-3 days, but it did not provide relief. She has a history of past surgery on her left arm, but does not think it is related to her current pain.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient reports that any movement of her left shoulder hurts. The doctor examines the patient and notes that the pain is exacerbated by movement. The doctor orders an x-ray, which shows no signs of a broken bone or fracture.  

RESULTS

  The x-ray results show no evidence of a fracture or broken bone. The doctor suspects that the patient has hurt her joint.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  Assessment: The patient has left shoulder pain that began after a gymnastics class and has not improved with Tylenol. The x-ray shows no signs of a fracture or broken bone.  Plan: The doctor recommends continuing to use pain relievers, specifically 600mg of ibuprofen 3 times a day for 1 week. The patient is advised to rest her left arm and avoid heavy lifting or using her left arm for at least 3-4 weeks. The doctor suggests using ice or a heat pad to relieve pain if it persists. The patient is to return in 3 weeks if the pain does not improve. The doctor also recommends trying Aleve (naproxen) as an alternative pain reliever.

Annual exam.

Regarding her depression, she has been going to therapy once a week for the past year. She reports that she is starting to go in person rather than virtual, which has helped even more. The patient has a good support system at home with her husband and her children live down the street.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Mr. James, a 65-year-old male, presented to the office with a 5-month history of constant pain in his left shoulder and elbow. He reported that the pain began after a fall 5 months ago while going up a flight of stairs. He stated that the pain is present all the time and can be quite severe at times. He has tried to continue his exercise routine, but the pain has worsened with lifting and has forced him to stop lifting weights. He has also tried icing the area, but has not taken any pain medication. He has been able to continue walking with his wife, but the pain has been a significant limitation.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's physical exam revealed tenderness to palpation over the left shoulder and elbow, with no warmth, erythema, or deformity. The Hawkins-Kennedy and Neer's tests were positive, indicating impingement syndrome of the left shoulder. The patient had normal proximal and distal upper extremity strength, intact median, radial, and ulnar sensation, and abduction to 90 degrees. The empty can test was normal.  

RESULTS

  The patient's laboratory and imaging results were not mentioned in the conversation.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient was diagnosed with impingement syndrome of the left shoulder. The plan was to start with physical therapy, with a referral to Dr. Martha Rivera for two sessions per week, as well as exercises to be done at home. If the physical therapy does not improve the patient's pain, a cortisone injection may be considered. The patient was also advised to continue icing the area and to rest as much as possible. The patient was instructed to follow up with the office if his symptoms worsen or do not improve with the therapy.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Randy Gutierrez, a 1-year-old male, presented to the clinic with a 1-day history of symptoms. His mother reported that he started feeling unwell around supper time last night, with a runny nose, stuffiness, and a cough. She also mentioned that he has been pulling on his ears and rubbing his nose excessively. The patient's temperature was 100.3°F (rectal). He had a low-grade fever, and his appetite was variable, with some interest in eating but also some refusal to eat. He had a normal energy level but was irritable and had difficulty sleeping. His mother reported that he had been exposed to his older sister, who had been sick, and also had a playdate with a nephew who had a sinus infection.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient was examined, and his ears, nose, and throat were checked. The doctor noted that the patient's ears looked okay, but the rest of the exam was not documented in detail.  

RESULTS

  The patient's vital signs were not documented, but his temperature was 100.3°F (rectal). The doctor ordered no laboratory tests or imaging studies.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient was diagnosed with a viral upper respiratory tract infection, likely a sinus infection. The doctor recommended that the patient continue to rest, drink plenty of fluids, and use saline mist in his nose. The doctor also prescribed a medication for the patient's sister, which was given to the patient as well, in case he developed a barky cough. The patient was instructed to use Zarbee's for his stuffy nose and to avoid giving him honey due to the risk of botulism. The doctor also recommended using a cradle cap shampoo, such as Head & Shoulders or Selsun Blue, to help manage the patient's cradle cap. The patient was scheduled to return to the clinic in one week for a follow-up appointment.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Randy Gutierrez is a 9-month-old male who is being evaluated today for a cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea. He is accompanied by his mother.

Symptoms began yesterday around dinnertime with clear nasal drainage, pulling on his ears bilaterally, and rubbing his nose frequently. He does not have a barky cough or a sore throat. His rectal temperature was 100.3 degrees. The patient did consume a 7 ounce bottle this morning, but mostly denied the next offered bottle and declined cereal. He is irritable and having trouble falling asleep, described “like he is fighting it”.

The patient has been exposed to an older sister who is sick and a cousin who has a sinus infection.

Treatment has included saline nasal spray, Tylenol, baby Vick's, and a humidifier. When the patient aroused this morning, he was warm to the touch. When his sleeper was removed, he was quite sweaty despite the air conditioner being on.

The patient's dandruff is also worsening. The only tried treatment is Aveeno shampoo.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Older sister.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Tylenol PRN.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Temperature 100.3 degrees.

Ears, Eyes, Nose, Throat
Nasal congestion, clear mucus, rhinorrhea. Bilateral pulling of ears, no sore throat or hoarseness.

Respiratory
Positive for cough.

ASSESSMENT

• Viral infection
• Dandruff

PLAN

Viral infection
The patient presents with a viral infection and does not need antibiotics currently. I advised continuation of fluids, rest. Additionally, I suggested trying Zarbee’s for children under 1 years old. If a croup cough develops, the patient will receive a 0.5 teaspoon.

I also provided counseling to the mother to avoid giving honey to the patient for his first year of life due to botulism spores.

Dandruff
The patient is experiencing worsening symptoms of dandruff. I recommended Head and Shoulders shampoo, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A-D 2 times per week, combined with his usual shampoo. I counseled the mother that she should be careful not to get the shampoo in the patient eyes and to not use the extra strength shampoos. Additionally, he may also use baby oil and a soft brush on the area.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return in one week for a re-check.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Kayla Ward, a 14-year-old female, presented to the office with a 2-year history of acne on her face, which is present most days. She has been using Persa-Gel and washing regularly, which has provided some relief. She denies any associated symptoms such as itching, bleeding, or pain. She has no additional past medical history and lives with her parents and sister. She is in 7th grade and participates in basketball, volleyball, and tap. She uses sunscreen with SPF 30 in the summer. Her mother reports that the acne flared when Kayla started school sports in the fall due to increased sweating.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The physical exam revealed multiple benign nevi on the trunk and extremities, scattered skin-colored papules, open and closed comedones, and erythematous papules on the face, primarily on the forehead and central cheeks and chin. The chest and back were relatively spared. The remainder of the examination was normal.  

RESULTS

  The results of the physical exam confirmed mild to moderate acne, mostly comedonal with a small inflammatory component.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  Assessment: Kayla has mild to moderate acne, mostly comedonal with a small inflammatory component.  Plan: I will start Kayla on a topical therapy regimen, which includes washing her face with a mild cleanser and applying a non-comedogenic moisturizer with sunscreen SPF 30 or higher in the morning. In the evening, she will wash her face with the same cleanser and apply adapalene 0.1% cream to the areas where she gets acne, starting with a few nights a week and gradually increasing to every night. She will also use Clean and Clear Persa-Gel in a thin layer and a non-comedogenic moisturizer. I will follow up with her in 6 months to assess her progress. I will also educate her on the potential side effects of adapalene, including sensitivity, dryness, and irritation, and instruct her to wear sunscreen when outside.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

New acne evaluation.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Kayla Ward is a 15-year-old female who presents for new patient evaluation of acne located on the face. She is accompanied by her mother today.

Kayla states her acne started approximately 2 years ago and it is present almost every day. The patient’s mother notes that the most significant acne flares started in the fall when she was playing school sports. It does not tend to flare with her periods. Kayla reports that today is a good day for her acne. She denies any significant acne present on the chest or back. There are no associated symptoms, including no itching, bleeding, or pain.

The patient has been washing her face regularly. Her acne regimen includes washing her face in the morning with Persa-Gel and at night e.l.f. SuperClarify Cleanser along with toner and Persa-Gel. This regimen is somewhat helpful. She wears sunscreen in the summer SPF 30.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Lives with parents and sister. They have a dog, bird, and rabbits in the home. She is in the 7th grade and active in basketball, volleyball, and tap dancing.

FAMILY HISTORY

No significant family medical history.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Integumentary
Full exam is performed today except for under the underwear and under the bra. There are multiple benign nevi on the trunk and extremities. Scattered skin-colored papules, open and closed comedones, and erythematous papules on the face, primarily on the forehead, with also some on the central cheeks and chin. The chest and back are relatively spared. The remainder of examination is normal.

ASSESSMENT

• Comedonal acne.
• Multiple benign melanocytic nevi of upper and lower extremities and trunk.

Kayla Ward is a 15-year-old female seen today for new acne evaluation. Full skin examination revealed mild to moderate comedonal acne with a small inflammatory component and multiple benign melanocytic nevi of upper and lower extremities and trunk.

PLAN

Comedonal acne.
We discussed the diagnosis, etiology, and treatment options. I recommend starting with topical therapy first. The patient was instructed to wash face every morning with a mild cleanser, then use a non-comedogenic moisturizer with sunscreen SPF 30 of higher. In the evening, wash face with same mild cleanser and allow to dry. Apply a thin layer of topical retinoid, Retin-A 0.1% gel, to acne prone areas. Start off using a few nights a week, slowly work up to using every night. If it is very expensive or not covered by insurance, you can try Differin gel over the counter. Your topical retinoid will cause sun sensitivity, so you will need to wear sunscreen when you are outside. It may also cause some dryness or irritation.

Multiple benign melanocytic nevi of upper and lower extremities and trunk.
Reassurance given moles are normal in appearance and have no concerning characteristics. Will continue to monitor for progression or changes in 6 months.

INSTRUCTIONS

Follow recommended cleaning regimen.
Start Retin-A 0.1% topical gel, apply to acne prone areas, titrate as instructed.
Follow up in 6 months.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Julia Jones, a 25-year-old female, presents for evaluation of breast reduction. She has been experiencing symptoms of macromastia, including back pain, neck pain, and bra strap indentations, for approximately 5-7 years. She has attempted nonsurgical treatments, including chiropractic care, but has not experienced significant relief. She is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty. Her minimum required resection, based on a body surface area (BSA) of 2.65, is 1,792 grams, which should be attainable. She has a history of breast cancer in her family, with her mother diagnosed at age 40. She has not had any mammograms yet, but plans to start at age 30. She is currently healthy, but has a history of migraines and takes propranolol for pain management. She is a non-smoker and occasional drinker.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  Physical exam findings reveal asymmetrical breasts with severe ptosis bilaterally. There is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally. No breast tenderness, masses, or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable. The patient's bra size is a G cup, and she experiences pain and discomfort when wearing a regular bra. She has bra strap indentations on her shoulders.  

RESULTS

  The patient's measurements were taken, and it was noted that one breast is slightly lower than the other. The patient's symptoms and physical exam findings are consistent with symptomatic macromastia.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty. The procedure will involve removing excess tissue from the breasts, lifting the nipples, and reducing the size of the breasts. The patient's minimum required resection is 1,792 grams, which should be attainable. The patient will have drains in place for approximately 1 week, and will need to avoid heavy lifting and driving for 2-3 weeks. The patient will also need to follow up with the office to answer any last-minute questions and review instructions before surgery. The patient will notify the office when her insurance information is available, and the office will submit the request for approval. Once approved, the patient will meet with the doctor again to review the surgery date and instructions.

Previous reconstruction: No.

Medical

Breasts
Inspection reveals asymmetrical breasts with severe ptosis bilaterally. There is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally. No breast tenderness, masses, or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable.

  Mrs. Wood, a 43-year-old female, presented to the office for a renal ultrasound due to hematuria. She reported passing two small kidney stones last week and experiencing episodes of bright red urine for the past six weeks. She described the urine as the color of cherry Kool-Aid. She also reported constant urination and severe pain (8/10) last week. She has since passed the stones and is currently pain-free. She has no drug allergies and is taking Alavert 10mg daily.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's abdomen was examined, and there was no flank pain or tenderness in the lower back. The urinalysis results were as follows: negative for leukocytes, nitrates, protein, ketone, bilirubin, and glucose; yellow and clear color; urobilinogen 0.2; pH 5.5; and specific gravity 1.020. The renal ultrasound revealed a 6mm non-obstructing left renal stone and normal right kidney.  

RESULTS

  The patient's urine dipstick results were negative for all parameters except for a trace amount of blood. The renal ultrasound revealed a 6mm non-obstructing left renal stone and normal right kidney. The patient's vital signs and physical exam were unremarkable.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient has a history of hematuria due to kidney stones. The patient has passed two stones and is currently pain-free. The patient has a 6mm non-obstructing left renal stone. The patient's risk factors for kidney stones include dehydration, family history, and dietary factors. The patient was advised to drink at least half of her body weight in ounces of water daily and avoid sugary drinks like Mountain Dew. She was also advised to avoid foods high in sodium oxalate and animal protein. The patient was given a handout with dietary recommendations and was instructed to follow up in a year unless she experiences any further issues. The patient was also advised to seek emergency room care if she experiences severe pain or bright red blood in her urine.

No surgical intervention is recommended, and the patient would like to discuss urolithiasis prevention. We had a very lengthy discussion with regards to urolithiasis formation and treating the underlying cause to prevent reoccurrence and associated potential dietary factors that could be involved with urolithiasis formation.

It is recommended that the patient maintain a healthy diet; limit salt, and animal protein. she should increase fluid consumption; adequate intake is half of her body weight in ounces of water daily and avoid sugary drinks. A copy of the dietary recommendations and avoidances was provided to the patient for reference. We will continue to monitor annually with renal ultrasound unless new symptoms develop. The patient voiced understanding and agreed with the recommended medical treatment plan.

INSTRUCTIONS

Follow up in 1 year with renal ultrasound, sooner should symptoms arise. Should you develop sudden onset of severe pain or gross hematuria go to the emergency room.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Diana Scott, a 100-year-old female, presented to the clinic on 7/9/2021 for a new patient visit. She was referred by her primary care physician (PCP) due to a new worrisome heart murmur. The patient reported that she has been experiencing severe leg pain for about a month, which has been managed with pain medication. She also reported feeling short of breath when walking too fast or too long, or when drinking a full glass of water without stopping. She denied chest pain, but mentioned that she has been experiencing lightheadedness and dizziness, which she manages with meclizine. The patient has a history of multiple surgeries and is taking several medications, including amlodipine, lisinopril, simvastatin, and levothyroxine. She has a family history of heart problems, including a son who had a heart attack and a brother who had a stroke.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The physical exam revealed a 4/6 systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border with mild radiation to the carotids. The patient's gait was normal for her age. The rest of the physical exam was unremarkable.  

RESULTS

  The patient's blood pressure was normal, and she was not experiencing any acute distress. The ECG showed sinus rhythm with frequent PVCs and an anterior septum infarct pattern. The patient's lipid profile was not available, but she was taking simvastatin for hypolipidemia.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient was diagnosed with a 4/6 systolic murmur at the upper right sternal border with mild radiation to the carotids, which is most likely representative of aortic stenosis. The patient's symptoms could be due to severe aortic stenosis, but her S2 is relatively preserved, which would be more consistent with moderate stenosis. The patient's echocardiogram will be ordered to assess her cardiac structure and function. Depending on the results, the patient may require further evaluation, including an ischemic evaluation or a heart monitor to assess the burden of her PVCs. The patient's hypolipidemia is managed by her PCP, and she may be able to stop her statin medication. The patient's lightheadedness and dizziness may be related to her aortic stenosis or PVCs, and will be evaluated further with the echocardiogram. The patient will follow up in 3-4 weeks to discuss the results and next steps.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Heart murmur.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Diana Scott is a 100-year-old female who presents today for evaluation of a heart murmur.

Ms. Scott has been recently diagnosed with a “new, worrisome” heart murmur. She denies chest pain, syncope, palpitations, fevers, or chills. She reports shortness of breath if she walks too fast, too long, or tries to drink a whole glass of water without stopping. The patient requires a walker and can walk around her cul-de-sac or to the end of the driveway without shortness of breath. Compared to 6 months ago, her functioning has declined 20-30%. Prior to 6 months, symptoms were absent. For the past month she has been experiencing severe, bilateral leg pain, which was limiting her mobility. She did see her doctor last week who completed a chest x-ray and prescribed pain medicine. Occasionally, she feels dizzy but takes medication for this and it subsides. Her ankles have been swelling for the past 6 months, but the swelling fluctuates day to day. Her weight has been slowly decreasing over the past year, with her current weight at 120 pounds, previous weight was 130 pounds. She also reports feeling “overly tired” but attributes this to her polypharmacy.

The patient is unsure if she has completed any cardiovascular testing.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Hyperlipidemia.
Hypertension.
Hypothyroid.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Requires a walker to ambulate.

FAMILY HISTORY

Son had a myocardial infarction.
Brother had a stroke and has diabetes.
Older sister has an unknown heart problem.
Family is positive for heart disease.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Levothyroxine.
Lisinopril.
Simvastatin 20 mg.
Meclizine.
Amlodipine.

ALLERGIES

No known drug allergies.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Constitutional
Elderly, no acute distress.

Cardiovascular
Normal S1, S2 preserved, irregular rhythm and normal rate. 4/6 systolic murmur at the RUSB with mild radiation to the carotids.

Musculoskeletal
Lower extremity edema

Neurologic
Gait normal for age.

RESULTS

EKG
Impression: Sinus rhythm with frequent PVCs as well as anterior septal infarct pattern.

ASSESSMENT

• Cardiac murmur.
• PVCs.
• Hypertension.
• Hyperlipidemia.
• Hypothyroid.
• Lightheadedness/dizziness.

PLAN

Cardiac murmur
Diana has a 4/6 systolic murmur at the RUSB with mild radiation to the carotids. This is most likely representative of aortic stenosis. She does have symptoms including a significant increase in fatigue over the last 6 months, shortness of breath, and some lightheadedness. This could be due to severe aortic stenosis, however, her S2 is relatively preserved which would be more consistent with moderate. She is unsure if she has had an echocardiogram, although some of her outside records do note diastolic dysfunction, so it is possible that she had one as some point in the past. We will order a repeat echocardiogram today to assess overall cardiac structure and function as well as valvular disease. If she does truly have severe aortic stenosis, I briefly discussed a TAVR procedure today as she is not a candidate for a surgical AVR given her age co-morbidities and frailty. She is unsure if she would be interested in this, but we will assess first with the echocardiogram and then discuss further at follow-up. She does not appear significantly volume overloaded today.

PVCs
Her referral mentions tachycardia. Her EKG today shows sinus rhythm with frequent PVCs as well as anterior septal infarct pattern. She does not have any significant palpitations. We will check an echocardiogram to assess overall cardiac structure and function. Depending on results as well as the severity of her aortic stenosis, we will need to consider an ischemic evaluation or possibly a heart monitor to assess the overall burden of PVCs. At this point, she is asymptomatic, so we will start with the echocardiogram only.

Hypertension
She is taking amlodipine and lisinopril. We will continue these as is.

Hyperlipidemia
This is managed by her PCP. She is on simvastatin 20 mg. She is having bilateral leg pain over the past month. This could be related to the simvastatin possibly and given her age, it would not be unreasonable to stop the simvastatin, but I will defer to her PCP. She has no known CAD but if we were to do a TAVR evaluation and this was discovered, a statin would be indicated.

Hypothyroidism
She takes levothyroxine for supplementation and will continue this as is.

Lightheadedness/dizziness
She is on meclizine for this. It is possible that some of her lightheadedness and dizziness are related to the aortic stenosis, and we will do an echocardiogram as noted above. She is also having some PVCs on her EKG, which could be contributing, but she does not feel palpitations. We will consider a monitor but start with the echocardiogram first as noted above.

INSTRUCTIONS

Follow-up in 3 to 4 weeks after her echocardiogram is done to discuss results and next steps.

Newly diagnosed allergic asthma.

She also reported a history of heavy alcohol use, but has decreased her consumption to 1-2 beers per day. The patient will undergo additional blood work to check for HCV RNA and HCV genotype, and an ultrasound to assess liver fibrosis. The patient will be scheduled to return in 3 weeks for follow-up. The patient's family members will also be screened for Hepatitis C.

  The patient is a [age]-year-old female who presents with a history of headaches that have been ongoing since her teenage years. Initially, the headaches were associated with her menstrual periods, but over time, they have become more frequent and severe. She reports that the headaches are located on the right side, behind her eye, and are described as a pulsing sensation. She has also noticed that the headaches worsen with exercise, particularly when she starts to jog. Additionally, she reports experiencing ringing in her ears and blurred vision, which is intermittent but worsening over the past few months.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's vital signs are within normal limits. The physical exam reveals papilledema (swelling behind the eye) on funduscopic exam. The neurological exam is intact, with normal cranial reflexes and no numbness or tingling in the hands or feet. The musculoskeletal exam is normal, with a normal gait.  

RESULTS

  The patient has not had a recent MRI of the brain, and the primary care physician recommended that she see a specialist before undergoing the test. The patient has not had any recent imaging studies to evaluate her symptoms.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient's symptoms are concerning for idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), a condition characterized by increased pressure in the brain without a known cause. The patient's symptoms, including worsening headaches with activity, ringing in the ears, and changes in vision, are consistent with this diagnosis. To confirm the diagnosis and rule out other conditions, the patient will undergo an MRI of the brain and a spinal tap to evaluate the pressure in the brain. The patient will also be referred to a neuro-ophthalmologist for a complete eye exam. Treatment options for IIH include medications to decrease intracranial pressure and lifestyle modifications, such as exercise and weight loss. The patient will be followed up with the results of the MRI and spinal tap, and further treatment will be determined based on the findings.

  The patient is diagnosed with moderate intermittent asthma. He also experiences numbness and tingling in his right leg, especially at night. He has tried physical therapy, strong pain medications, and has been considered for surgery, but has avoided it. He reports that his back pain has limited his activities, including his recent interest in playing golf. He denies any family history of spine conditions and has been a non-smoker for 20 years.

  The patient's vital signs are within normal limits. The musculoskeletal back exam reveals midline tenderness at L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness. The patient experiences pain with lumbar flexion and supine straight leg test is positive. The neurological examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome. The patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.  

RESULTS

  The patient's MRI results show disk herniation with nerve impingement at L4-L5.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient's symptoms are consistent with an L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy. The doctor recommends a spinal injection with a corticosteroid to help reduce inflammation and alleviate pain. The procedure will be performed under fluoroscopy to localize the exact area of pain. The patient will be monitored for response to the injection, and the procedure can be repeated up to 4 times a year if necessary. The patient is scheduled to receive the first injection today.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

He reported that he was water skiing on a boat on Lake Martin and twisted his knee while landing a jump, resulting in pain and swelling. He denied hearing or feeling anything pop at the time of injury. He has been taking over-the-counter Aleve twice a day and applying a cold pack to the affected area, which has provided some relief. He has a history of hypertension, for which he is currently taking lisinopril 20mg daily, and has been managing his blood pressure well. He has no history of previous knee injuries or surgeries.

The patient reported pain to palpation of the right medial knee, and there was decreased range of motion with varus and valgus testing. The Lachman sign was negative, and the dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were palpable.  

RESULTS

  The patient's blood pressure was 120/77 in the office, which is within normal limits. The lipid panel and tetanus shot will be ordered as part of the patient's health maintenance.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient was diagnosed with a medial collateral ligament strain of the right knee. An x-ray of the right knee will be ordered to rule out any fractures. The patient will be referred to physical therapy to strengthen the muscles around the knee and prevent further injury. The patient will be prescribed meloxicam 15mg daily to reduce swelling and pain. The patient will be advised to rest the knee for a while to avoid further injury. The patient's hypertension will be managed with continued lisinopril 20mg daily, and a lipid panel will be ordered to check for any abnormalities. A tetanus shot will be administered as part of the patient's health maintenance.

However, a chest X-ray revealed a lung nodule, which was confirmed by a CT scan. The patient was referred to our clinic for further evaluation. The patient reports that the cough started after a physically demanding activity of loading horses, and he has a history of smoking, which he quit 7 years ago. He also has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate. The patient is an avid biker and has a history of exercising regularly.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  Vital signs: BP 124/76, HR 70, RR 16, O2 sat 98% on room air, afebrile. Physical exam revealed clear and equal bilateral lung sounds, regular heart rate and rhythm, no clicks, rubs, or murmurs, and no edema to the lower extremities. The patient has some swelling to the knuckles, which is consistent with his history of rheumatoid arthritis.  

RESULTS

  The patient had a chest CT scan that showed a solitary 2-cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe. The nodule is smooth in appearance and no emphysematous disease is present. The patient's pulmonary function test (PFT) results are pending.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule. Given the patient's history of smoking, the nodule is concerning for malignancy. The plan is to perform a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active. If the nodule is active, a video-assisted thoracoscopy will be performed to remove the nodule and a small portion of the lung. The patient will be under general anesthesia and will require a chest tube for 1 day post-procedure. The patient will be admitted to the hospital for 1 night and discharged the following day. The patient will also continue to follow up with his rheumatologist and continue his medication therapy as previously outlined.

Here is a clinical note summarizing the conversation:  

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  Jerry, a 45-year-old male, presented to the office today with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his 10-year-old son. He reported that his son, who is a big fan of Steph Curry, is more aggressive than his peers and accidentally collided with Jerry while he was attempting a shot. Jerry landed awkwardly and twisted his ankle, resulting in bruising, swelling, and instability. He has been experiencing difficulty walking and is concerned about disrupting his recent progress in working out.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The physical exam revealed exquisite tenderness on the lateral side of the ankle, with some tenderness over the medial deltoid region. The ankle was swollen, but skin integrity was intact, and capillary refill was brisk. Jerry reported difficulty walking on the ankle due to instability and pain. The doctor performed a quick exam, noting that Jerry was able to stand on the ankle but was unable to walk on it.  

RESULTS

  X-rays of the ankle revealed a displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures. The fracture is considered unstable, which explains Jerry's symptoms of instability and pain.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The diagnosis is a lateral malleolar fracture. Given the location and instability of the fracture, surgery is recommended to ensure proper healing. Jerry will require a plate and screws to stabilize the fracture. He will be out for approximately 3 months, which may impact his ability to coach his son's basketball team starting in April. In the meantime, Jerry will be prescribed meloxicam to reduce swelling, and he will need to ice and elevate the ankle regularly. He will also require crutches to avoid putting weight on the ankle. The doctor will follow up with Jerry to monitor his progress and adjust the treatment plan as needed.

He experiences pain and feelings of instability within the ankle while ambulating and is concerned for further injury.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient works from home and plans to coach his son’s basketball team in 04/2022. He enjoys working out and reports he recently resumed engaging in a consistent gym routine.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right ankle pain and instability.
Skin: Reports right ankle bruising and swelling.

PHYSICAL EXAM

CV: Brisk capillary refill.
SKIN: No epidermolysis. Intact.
MSK: Examination of the right ankle: Exquisite tenderness laterally. Mild tenderness over the medial deltoid region. Swelling on the lateral side of the ankle. No horrible malalignment.

RESULTS

X-rays, including AP, lateral, and oblique views of the right ankle, were obtained in the office and reviewed today. These demonstrate a displaced lateral malleolus at the Weber C level. There is no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.

ASSESSMENT

Right lateral malleolar fracture.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with him regarding treatment options. Recommendation was made for an open reduction internal fixation of the right ankle. We discussed the risks and benefits of the procedure as well as the postoperative recovery period following surgery. I advised that it will be 3 months before he can resume his exercise routine. In the meantime, I will prescribe meloxicam to reduce swelling. I recommended he ice and elevate the ankle as well. He will be given crutches to assist with ambulation.

The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  A 54-year-old male patient presents with a 2-3 month history of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheekbone area of his face. The pain is described as excruciating and is often triggered by certain temperatures or cold weather. The patient reports that the pain can last from a few minutes to an hour and is not accompanied by any sensory loss, weakness, or vision changes. He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and tiger balm without relief. His family doctor referred him to a neurosurgeon, but he was seen by the current doctor instead.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's physical exam is notable for a lack of sensory loss or weakness in the right side of his face. He is able to follow the doctor's finger and show his teeth symmetrically. He reports a little numbness on the right side of his face when the doctor runs cotton tape along the side of his face. The doctor uses a needle to poke the patient's face and finds that the sensation is dull on the right side and sharp on the left side.  

RESULTS

  The patient's MRI shows a small blood vessel abutting and possibly pinching the trigeminal nerve, which is a nerve that provides sensory innervation to the face. The doctor suspects that the patient may have trigeminal neuralgia, a condition in which nerve compression causes shooting electrical pain in the face.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient is diagnosed with trigeminal neuralgia and is started on tegretol, a medication that can help reduce the excitability of the nerve and alleviate pain. The doctor explains the potential side effects of the medication, including memory loss, tingling, and skin reactions. The patient is also scheduled for genetic testing to ensure that the medication will be safe for him. The plan is to try the tegretol for a month and then follow up to assess its effectiveness. If the medication does not provide adequate relief, the patient will be considered for surgical options to decompress the nerve and relieve the compression.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  The patient, a 4-year-old mother of twins, presented to the office with a complaint of a knee injury sustained while skiing in Vermont last weekend. She reported catching her ski and falling, resulting in a loud pop and subsequent pain in her knee. She was unable to bear weight and was taken down the mountain by ski patrol. She has been experiencing pain on the outside of her knee, which feels like it won't hold her weight, and has difficulty bending and straightening her knee. She has been taking ibuprofen for pain relief, which has provided some relief.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's physical exam revealed:  * Edema and effusion over the right knee * Pain to palpation of the right lateral knee * Decreased flexion and extension of the right knee * Positive Lachman sign * Palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse * No leg edema in the ankle  

RESULTS

  The patient had an X-ray of her right knee, which did not show any bony abnormalities. However, the doctor is concerned that she may have ruptured her anterior cruciate ligament (ACL).  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The doctor's assessment is that the patient may have a ruptured ACL, which is a major ligament that helps connect and move the knee. To confirm this diagnosis, the doctor ordered a knee MRI to be done in the next couple of days. In the meantime, the patient will continue to take ibuprofen for pain relief and will use a knee brace to support her knee. The doctor will discuss the results of the MRI and potential treatment options, including grafting, at a follow-up appointment.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right knee injury

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Michelle King is a pleasant 44-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of a right knee injury. Her injury was sustained while skiing in Vermont last weekend after she caught her ski when coming off of the lift and fell. She reports a pop in her knee at the time of injury and has been experiencing pain since that time. Due to her fall she was unable to weight bear and had to be taken down by the ski patrol. She locates her pain to the lateral aspect of her knee and also describes the feeling of instability as well as buckling. At this time she is experiencing difficulty with knee flexion; however, she states this was not a problem immediately after the injury. Full knee extension is also quite painful for her. The patient denies any numbness or tingling in her toes. She is currently utilizing crutches for ambulation which were provided to her by the ski patrol. In terms of pain control, she is taking ibuprofen which is providing some relief.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports she is otherwise healthy and denies a history of high blood pressure or diabetes.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient reports she is very active with skiing and taking care of her 4-year-old twins. She mentions that she would like to get back into running at some point. She states that her husband is home with her, but he also works a job where he has gone quite a bit.

MEDICATIONS

Patient reports she is taking ibuprofen.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right knee pain and limited range of motion.
Neurological: Denies numbness or tingling in toes.

PHYSICAL EXAM

GAIT: The patient is ambulating with crutches.
CV: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmur.
RESPIRATORY: Lungs are clear to auscultation.
MSK: Examination of the right knee: Edema and effusion noted. Pain with palpation of the lateral knee. Decreased flexion and extension. Positive Lachman's. Palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse. No ankle edema.

RESULTS

4 views of the right knee were taken today. These reveal no bony abnormalities.

ASSESSMENT

Right knee pain, possible ACL tear.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to her current symptoms. I have recommended that we obtain an MRI of the right knee to evaluate for a possible ACL tear. In the meantime, I have advised her to continue taking ibuprofen 600 to 800 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain. She will also be placed into a brace for increased knee support.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me once the MRI results are available for review and further discussion.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

  The patient, a male, presented to the clinic with complaints of neck pain. He reported being in multiple car crashes, with the most recent one occurring in February of this year. He stated that he had been experiencing pain in his neck since the accident and rated his pain as a 12 out of 10. He reported that he had been prescribed fentanyl in the past but had not received a prescription for several weeks. He also mentioned that he had been to therapy and a pain clinic. The patient's lawyer had recommended that he see a doctor.  

PHYSICAL EXAM

  The patient's physical exam revealed pain on palpation of the neck, both on the bony process and the muscle. He had a moderate range of motion in his neck, but bending forward and backward caused significant pain. There was no bruising, swelling, or laceration noted on the neck. The patient reported fatigue and numbness in his left arm and right leg.  

RESULTS

  The patient's imaging results, including an x-ray, did not show a fracture. The doctor ordered an MRI to further evaluate the patient's condition.  

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

  The patient was diagnosed with a neck sprain. The doctor explained that the pain could be explained by multiple factors, but fortunately, it was not a broken neck. The plan was to treat the patient conservatively, starting with a medication called robaxin to help relax the muscles and reduce tension. The patient was instructed to take the medication 6-8 hours apart and to use ice and heat as needed. The doctor also referred the patient to physical therapy and mentioned the possibility of local injections and pain medication if the robaxin was not effective. The patient was given a work excuse due to his inability to move and was told to wait for the MRI results before considering further treatment.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Neck pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Brandon Green is a pleasant 46-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of neck pain. His pain began when he was involved in a motor vehicle accident in 02/2022 when he was on his way home from a pain clinic. The patient notes that he has been in 4 motor vehicle accidents; however, he notes that he was fine after the first two accidents, but the third motor vehicle accident is when his neck and back pain began. He states that he was in therapy following the second accident and had surgery after his third accident. The patient was seen at a pain clinic secondary to neck and back pain. He was prescribed fentanyl; however, he has not received a prescription for several weeks. Today, he reports that his pain is a 12 out of 10. He describes his pain as sharp and incapacitating with stiffness and pain. The patient also reports headaches, occasional dizziness. He denies any recent visual disturbances. He also reports numbness in his left arm and right leg. The patient also reports spasms throughout his body. He states that he has been experiencing fatigue since the accident. He notes that he is unable to work with this much pain.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports fatigue.
Eyes: Denies any recent visual disturbances.
Musculoskeletal: Reports neck and back pain, and occasional swelling and bruising of the neck.
Neurological: Reports headaches, dizziness, spasms, and numbness.

PHYSICAL EXAM

SKIN: No lacerations.
MSK: Examination of the cervical spine: Pain on palpation on the bony process and muscle. Moderate ROM. No bruising or edema noted.

RESULTS

X-rays of the neck reveal no fractures.

ASSESSMENT

Neck sprain.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his x-rays did not reveal any signs of a fracture. I recommended an MRI for further evaluation. I have also prescribed the patient Robaxin 1500 mg every 6 to 8 hours to treat his pain. I have also advised him to utilize ice, a heating pad, IcyHot, or Biofreeze on his neck as needed. I have also provided him with a home exercise program to work on his range of motion. I advised the patient that he will not be able to work until we have the MRI results.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me after his MRI for results.
